chapters.title: The weight a single speech can carry
chapters.text:
From raw honesty to shameless posturing, the most famous speeches in history stir up all kinds of feelings. This summary pulls together a wide range of speeches, from the soaring hope of John F. Kennedy's inaugural address to the hard lessons of those who saw true evil, like Elie Wiesel. Read on to see how great orators harness the power of language.

There are many ways to write and deliver a speech. Susan B. Anthony asked a question with deep historical weight in her speech: are women persons? That single question showed just how far she had to go to undo the unfair treatment of certain races and groups. Indira Gandhi, in her speech The Responsibility of Women in India, insisted that society should treat women as equal citizens.

Even tyrants have their own style. Adolf Hitler, in a speech titled My Patience Is Now at an End, I Am From Now On Just First Soldier of the Reich, dressed his views up as righteous political outrage, even though everyone knew he was lying. Joseph Stalin, in a speech titled It Is Imperative that We Agree to Conclude the Pact, was blunt about his views, no matter how cruel or ugly they were. The contrast with Hitler's style is sharp.

By studying the speeches of famous figures, you can understand the time they lived in much better.

This summary gathers some of the most historically important talks. You will see the values and the flaws of the people in those times. You will also see the skill and craft that went into writing and delivering these speeches.

chapters.title: Sermons that started a religion
chapters.text:
Jesus and Mohammed are two of the most influential figures in history. Born in the first century in Judaea, Jesus lived among the Jewish people and grew popular for his unique teachings.

The Jewish state religion at the time was Judaism. The authorities spread the idea that Jesus was a threat, because he criticized their leaders and called himself the Son of God.

Great leaders free people from tyranny and face fierce pushback from tyrants.

There are many views of Jesus as a historical figure. He has been called a reformer, a justice fighter, and a revolutionary. For Christians, he is a prophet with a special link to God, sent to bring salvation to humanity.

Jesus was a moral teacher, and his Sermon on the Mount became the backbone of Christianity. It laid out the ethical principles for how people should treat one another. Unlike a speech for a single occasion, it is a collection of short, instructive sayings.

Blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy.

At the time of this speech, Jewish society lived under hostility. Mercy and forgiveness were abstract ideas, and Jesus' teaching was a radical break from the norm.

Jesus told his followers to show compassion to one another, which was another idea that did not fit first-century Jewish culture.

After a speech that led to hatred and death, Jesus of Nazareth shared new teachings with his Jewish followers and laid the groundwork for a new religion. Christians did not fully accept his words right away, but they eventually became the foundation of one of the world's biggest religions.

Another powerful figure in history is the Prophet Mohammed from Western Arabia. Like Jesus, he taught his followers in his own way and put personal devotion to God and a life of service at the center.

Mohammed's mission began at age forty, with a vision of the angel Gabriel, who gave him the first of many divine messages. Mohammed began to share his teachings, and, just as with Jesus, some believed in him and others did not. The famous prophet praised Allah and urged others to do good. He taught that wherever a traveler goes, the traveler should turn toward the Sacred Mosque, because Allah sees where you go and what you do.

In another part of his teaching, there are clear instructions on how to pray, in a way that mirrors Jesus' own words.

Mohammed's teachings and sayings were gathered into the Qur'an after his death.

chapters.title: Speeches and the fight for equal rights
chapters.text:
Susan Brownell Anthony was a Massachusetts-born teacher who switched careers to become an activist in the mid-1800s. The country was in the middle of a civil war, and popular movements were springing up. Anthony wanted to be a voice for those who were oppressed, pushed aside, and ignored.

She is famous for her speeches on the unfair treatment of African Americans and the hateful rule of men that kept women in lower social positions.

She cast a vote in the 1872 presidential election to test how the law was interpreted. Police arrested her for voting illegally. That act led to her famous speech "Are women persons?" In it, she appealed to a higher authority than the state. She put the state itself on trial for its crimes against women, who she said were just as much God's creatures as men.

Anthony argued that the Declaration of Independence and the US Constitution could not hand people the rights that came from God. All they could do was protect how people lived out those rights.

To treat women as less is to tear apart the fabric that holds society together.

To make her case, she walked through the word "people" in the Constitution and showed that it gave clear room for women to vote. She risked her life to force the state to recognize women as part of the people the government serves. Her point was simple. If women are human, they have the right to vote. If they cannot vote, the state is saying they are not persons. That made it much harder for those in power to keep denying women the vote.

This speech became a sharp and effective weapon in the fight for women's rights. Thanks to the courage and the long work of Susan Brownell Anthony, women in America now have the same right to vote as men. Anthony did not live to see that day, but she kept campaigning into her seventies. She remains a towering figure in the fight for women's rights.

chapters.title: How an inauguration can spark hope
chapters.text:
John F. Kennedy was born in 1917 in Massachusetts to a thriving Irish-American family. After graduating from Harvard, he joined the US Navy and earned a medal for bravery. He served in the House of Representatives and the Senate, then became the youngest president in American history.

Kennedy was a master of eloquent speech and narrowly won the presidency over Richard Nixon. His quiet work to help free Martin Luther King from jail won him strong support from African American communities.

Kennedy was deeply concerned about America's place in the world. His speech was about restoring hope and calling on Americans to work together and build the nation.

He said that together they could correct the mistakes of the past and move toward a happy and free life. The new president inspired faith by promising to explore the stars and the oceans and to seek cures for diseases that had no cure.

In his view, America had lost some of its influence in the world under his predecessor, President Eisenhower. He wanted the world to know that America was back.

Persuading people to chase an impossible goal takes courage, and getting there brings great glory.

He sent a clear message to friends and rivals alike that the spirit of the first American revolution was alive again. That spirit filled the country with confidence, pride, and joy. He promised bold steps to put the country back on top. He declared, for example, that America would be the first to reach the moon. That single goal pulled the country together, and reaching the moon proved America could still do great things.

He also spoke about economic reform and argued that the rich only survive when the poor are lifted up too.

Kennedy ended with a call to action. He challenged Americans to give what they could to the country's progress instead of asking what they could take. He challenged other countries to put the freedom of humanity above their own narrow interests.

According to Simon Montefiore, President Kennedy spent about two months preparing his famous line: "Ask not what your country can do for you."

chapters.title: We are human first, scientists second
chapters.text:
Julius Robert Oppenheimer entered the world in 1904, in New York City. He cut his teeth on physics at Harvard, then moved across the Atlantic to the Cavendish Laboratory in Cambridge to study quantum mechanics and relativity. He wrapped up his training with a PhD at the University of Gottingen in Germany.

When the US threw its weight into the Allied fight against Nazi Germany in 1941, President Roosevelt tapped Oppenheimer to run the Manhattan Project. The political and military leaders were terrified that Germany would reach a scientific breakthrough first, so they poured money and talent into a parallel effort. Physicists from Britain and the United States joined forces to crack open nuclear energy and turn it into a weapon.

The team pulled it off, and in August 1945, the order came to use the bomb in the real world. President Truman greenlit the dropping of atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. The damage far exceeded what the scientists had forecast.

Standing in the rubble of what they had built, Oppenheimer insisted that the bombs had made global cooperation more urgent than ever. He did not feel guilty about his role in building nuclear weapons, but he never dodged his moral share of the blame either.

Oppenheimer pushed back hard against the push to build an even deadlier hydrogen bomb. Washington yanked his security clearance because it suspected him of working with the Soviets. Even so, he kept speaking out about the peaceful uses of atomic power.

The question is not only how to build something. The question is whether we should build it at all. The fact that we can do a thing does not mean we should.

In his own address, Oppenheimer tackled exactly that question. He made the case that atomic weapons had turned into a problem for both scientists and the rest of the planet. It was the same kind of problem as Nazism, a problem that can only be defeated by people working together. He called atomic weapons a clear threat to human survival. He called for firm control over nuclear arsenals and insisted that everyone share the weight of the damage already done.

Oppenheimer hoped his words would stop the world from building even more terrible weapons. He spent the final stretch of his life digging into the relationship between science and the rest of society.

chapters.title: Indifference is its own form of evil
chapters.text:
Elie Wiesel's speech is one of the most powerful of the last century. Wiesel was a Romanian American of Jewish descent who warned about the dangers of an uncaring society. He was just a boy when Nazi soldiers invaded his hometown of Sighet and deported Jews to be "cleansed."

In 1944, the Nazis sent Wiesel and his family to Auschwitz-Birkenau. He never saw his mother or his sister again. Brutal labor and starvation killed his father just a few weeks before the camp was liberated by the US Third Army in 1945. Wiesel's speech "The Peril of Indifference" gives heartfelt thanks to the American soldiers who broke their chains. He was grateful for their righteous anger and their genuine sympathy. He then asks the audience what they will remember about the twentieth century. Wiesel argued that indifference would be the century's defining feature, and that the future would judge the era for it. People chose detachment and apathy because it was comfortable and felt safe. Few thought about the terrible cost of refusing to stand up for others.

In Wiesel's view, human beings are meant to care for one another. Indifference is just a way to prove you have lost your humanity.

Wiesel asked his audience what indifference really is. Is it an ideal, a philosophy, a virtue? It is easier to look away from the pain of others, because then you can get on with your own life. Indifference protects your peace of mind, which is exactly why it feels so tempting. Yet it strips people of their empathy. It is worse than anger or hatred, because it has nothing behind it, just emptiness.

Indifference is complicity with the people who commit evil.

An apathetic person does nothing to fight evil, because they have stopped telling right from wrong. Indifference needs a dead conscience to thrive, and it gives evildoers free space to act without fear. It magnifies the pain of the victims. Humanity has to do everything it can to push back against indifference in the new century.

Has the human being become less indifferent and more human? Have we really learned from our experiences?

chapters.title: Final thoughts and a way to practice
chapters.text:
Words have real power when they are chosen well. A picture may be worth a thousand words, but we have seen how a thousand well-chosen words, in the mouth of a skilled speaker, can launch a movement that changes the world.

Jesus Christ, Prophet Mohammed, Susan B. Anthony, and John F. Kennedy all used their words to challenge the people of their time. They called for high ethical standards, gave a voice to the oppressed, and offered people a higher purpose to live for. Their themes can help you rally people in your own circle around a just cause. If you ever need to give a speech, you can study the words of these great orators and borrow from their ability to move an audience.

Great speeches can push people in any direction you want them to go. Build real communication skills and put them to work in your personal and professional life.

Great speeches share a few things in common.

They are built to be easy to remember, so the audience can hold onto the main point.

They speak to the needs of listeners. A great speech can reach the desire for love, the pull of anger, the pull to aspire, or the sting of being called out for a wrong choice.

Try this next.

Great speeches can take weeks to prepare, or they can spill out of a passionate speaker in a single burst. Either way, preparation is what makes an impromptu speech work.

Look again at the most striking quotes from the orators in this summary.

Write them out and post them where you can see them.

Memorize the ones that speak to what you need right now.

If you made it to the end, well done.
